_comment: VERNUX Pattern Library — built by tools/build_db.py

patterns.description_noob: I'll update all your installed apps and tools to their latest versions.
patterns.description_learner: pkg update = refresh package list, pkg upgrade = install newer versions of everything installed.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install Python 3 — the most popular coding language. Takes about 30 seconds.
patterns.description_learner: In Termux the package is called python3, not python. pkg install fetches and installs it.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install Git — the tool you need to work with GitHub and save your project history.
patterns.description_learner: Git is version control. After install, you'll need to configure your name and email before committing.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install Node.js — used for JavaScript projects and web servers.
patterns.description_learner: Termux package is 'nodejs' not 'node'. Includes npm automatically.

patterns.description_noob: I'll ask Android for permission to access your phone's storage (Downloads, Pictures, etc). A popup will appear — tap Allow.
patterns.description_learner: termux-setup-storage creates a ~/storage symlink to Android storage locations. Must be run once. Accept the popup.

patterns.description_noob: I'll copy your file to the Downloads folder on your phone — where you can find it in the Files app.
patterns.description_learner: Termux home is sandboxed. /sdcard/Download/ is your phone's Downloads folder accessible to Android apps.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show you exactly where you are in the file system — like checking which room you're in.
patterns.description_learner: pwd = print working directory. Shows your full current path.

triggers: what files are in this folder
patterns.description_noob: I'll show you all the files and folders here, including hidden ones.
patterns.description_learner: ls -l = detailed list, -a = include hidden files (starting with dot).

patterns.description_noob: I'll create a new folder for you.
patterns.description_learner: mkdir -p creates the folder and any missing parent folders. Safe to run even if it already exists.

patterns.description_noob: I'll permanently delete that file. This cannot be undone — there is no Recycle Bin.
patterns.description_learner: rm removes a single file permanently. Use rm -r for folders. No undo.

patterns.description_noob: I'll bundle your folder into a single compressed zip file.
patterns.description_learner: zip -r = recursive (includes subfolders). Output is a .zip archive you can share.

patterns.description_noob: I'll unpack the zip file and put all the files into a folder.
patterns.description_learner: unzip extracts to current directory. Use -d folder to extract to a specific location.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show how much storage space is left on your device.
patterns.description_learner: df -h = disk free, human readable. ~ = your Termux home directory.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show how much memory (RAM) your device has and how much is currently being used.
patterns.description_learner: free -h shows total, used, and available RAM in human-readable format.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install {package} for you. This might take a minute depending on your connection.
patterns.description_learner: pkg install is Termux's package manager. -y auto-confirms. Package name must be the exact Termux name.

patterns.description_noob: I'll download the entire project from GitHub to your Termux.
patterns.description_learner: git clone downloads a repository. Creates a folder with the project name. Requires git installed.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show you which files have changed since your last save.
patterns.description_learner: git status shows tracked/untracked changes. Run this before committing.

patterns.description_noob: I'll download that file from the internet to your current folder.
patterns.description_learner: wget downloads a file from a URL. Use -O filename.ext to save with a specific name.

patterns.description_noob: I'll run your Python script.
patterns.description_learner: python3 executes a .py file. Make sure you're in the right directory first.

patterns.description_noob: I'll add a Python library that you can use in your scripts.
patterns.description_learner: pip is Python's package manager. Installs libraries from PyPI. Use pip3 if pip isn't found.

patterns.description_noob: I'll clean up the screen so it's blank again.
patterns.description_learner: clear scrolls the terminal. Your history is still accessible by scrolling up.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show you all the programs running right now in Termux.
patterns.description_learner: ps aux = process snapshot. a=all users, u=user-friendly, x=include background processes.

patterns.description_noob: I'll stop that running program. You'll need the process ID number — use 'show processes' first to find it.
patterns.description_learner: kill sends SIGTERM to a process by PID. Use kill -9 if it doesn't respond.

patterns.description_noob: I'll move you into that folder.
patterns.description_learner: cd = change directory. Use cd ~ to go home, cd .. to go up one level, cd - to go back.

patterns.description_noob: I'll display the contents of that file right here in the terminal.
patterns.description_learner: cat prints file contents to stdout. Use less {file} for long files you can scroll.

patterns.description_noob: I'll check if your internet connection is working.
patterns.description_learner: ping sends 4 packets to Google's DNS (8.8.8.8). If you see replies, internet is working.

patterns.description_noob: I'll give that script permission to run.
patterns.description_learner: chmod +x adds execute permission. Then run it with ./{script}

patterns.description_noob: I'll install Nano — a simple text editor you can use right inside Termux.
patterns.description_learner: nano is the most beginner-friendly terminal text editor. Open a file: nano filename.txt. Ctrl+X to exit.

patterns.description_noob: I'll search for available packages that match what you're looking for.
patterns.description_learner: pkg search queries the Termux package index. Shows all matching package names.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install FFmpeg — a powerful tool for working with video and audio files.
patterns.description_learner: Termux package is 'ffmpeg' not 'ffmpeg4'. Includes full codec support.

patterns.description_noob: I'll set up this folder as a Git project so you can track your changes.
patterns.description_learner: git init creates a .git folder here — this becomes a local repository.

patterns.description_noob: I'll mark all your changed files to be saved in the next snapshot.
patterns.description_learner: git add . stages all changes in the current directory. Run git status after to verify.

patterns.description_noob: I'll save a snapshot of your work with a short description.
patterns.description_learner: git commit -m saves staged changes with a message. Always stage files first with git add.

patterns.description_noob: I'll upload your saved work to GitHub so it's backed up online.
patterns.description_learner: git push sends your local commits to the remote repository on GitHub.

patterns.description_noob: I'll download the latest changes from GitHub to your device.
patterns.description_learner: git pull fetches and merges remote changes into your current branch.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show the last 10 saved snapshots of your project.
patterns.description_learner: git log --oneline shows compact commit history. -10 limits to last 10.

patterns.description_noob: I'll create a private Python environment just for this project so packages don't clash.
patterns.description_learner: python3 -m venv creates an isolated Python environment. Activate with: source venv/bin/activate

patterns.description_noob: I'll switch into your project's private Python environment.
patterns.description_learner: source venv/bin/activate activates the venv. Your prompt changes to show (venv). Use deactivate to exit.

patterns.description_noob: I'll rename that file or folder for you.
patterns.description_learner: mv is used for both moving and renaming. The original file name changes to the new one.

patterns.description_noob: I'll make a copy of that file.
patterns.description_learner: cp copies a file. Use cp -r for folders. Original stays untouched.

patterns.description_noob: I'll search all files in this folder for that word or phrase.
patterns.description_learner: grep -r searches recursively through all files. Use -i for case-insensitive.

patterns.description_noob: I'll search for that file in this folder and all subfolders.
patterns.description_learner: find . searches the current directory recursively. -name filters by filename. Use * as wildcard.

patterns.description_noob: I'll extract the contents of that compressed archive.
patterns.description_learner: tar -xzf extracts a .tar.gz file. x=extract, z=gzip, f=filename. Adds -C dir/ to extract elsewhere.

patterns.description_noob: I'll compress your folder into a .tar.gz archive.
patterns.description_learner: tar -czf creates a compressed archive. c=create, z=gzip, f=filename.

patterns.description_noob: I'll start a local web server on port 8080. Open your browser to http://localhost:8080 to see your files.
patterns.description_learner: python3 -m http.server serves the current directory over HTTP. Port 8080 by default. Ctrl+C to stop.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show your device's IP addresses on the local network.
patterns.description_learner: ip addr show displays network interfaces. We grep for 'inet ' to show only IPv4 addresses.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install Zsh and Oh My Zsh — this gives your terminal a much nicer look and useful features.
patterns.description_learner: Installs zsh shell then Oh My Zsh framework. Adds themes, plugins, and auto-completion. Will prompt to set zsh as default shell.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install tmux — it lets you run multiple terminal sessions and keeps things running even if you close Termux.
patterns.description_learner: tmux is a terminal multiplexer. Sessions survive Termux being closed. Split panes with Ctrl+B then %.

patterns.description_noob: I'll tell Android to keep Termux running even when your screen turns off.
patterns.description_learner: termux-wake-lock prevents Android from killing Termux in the background. Needs termux-api installed.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show you the 20 biggest files and folders in your home directory, sorted by size.
patterns.description_learner: du -sh gets size of each item, sort -rh sorts by human-readable size descending, head -20 limits output.

patterns.description_noob: I'll connect this project to your GitHub repository.
patterns.description_learner: git remote add origin sets the remote URL. Run after git init to connect to GitHub.

patterns.description_noob: I'll rename the default branch to 'main' — the modern GitHub standard.
patterns.description_learner: git branch -M main renames the current branch to main. GitHub default is now 'main' not 'master'.

patterns.description_noob: I'll upload your project to GitHub for the first time and connect the branches.
patterns.description_learner: git push -u origin main sets the upstream tracking. After this, just 'git push' works.

patterns.description_noob: I'll undo your last save but keep all your file changes intact.
patterns.description_learner: git reset --soft HEAD~1 moves HEAD back one commit but keeps changes staged. --mixed unstages them too.

patterns.description_noob: I'll throw away the changes to that file and restore it to how it was in your last save.
patterns.description_learner: git checkout -- file restores a file to its last committed state. Cannot be undone.

patterns.description_noob: I'll temporarily hide your unsaved changes so you can do something else, then bring them back later.
patterns.description_learner: git stash saves uncommitted changes to a stack. git stash pop restores them.

patterns.description_noob: I'll bring back the changes you temporarily hid.
patterns.description_learner: git stash pop restores the most recent stash and removes it from the stack.

patterns.description_noob: I'll create an SSH key — a secure way to connect to GitHub without typing your password every time.
patterns.description_learner: ssh-keygen creates a public/private key pair. ed25519 is modern and secure. Add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub to GitHub.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show your public SSH key so you can copy it to GitHub.
patterns.description_learner: Prints your ed25519 public key. Copy the full output and add it to GitHub → Settings → SSH Keys.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show all the Python libraries installed on your device.
patterns.description_learner: pip list shows all installed packages with versions. pip freeze > requirements.txt exports them.

patterns.description_noob: I'll save a list of all your Python packages to a file so others can install the same ones.
patterns.description_learner: pip freeze > requirements.txt exports installed packages. Share this file and others run pip install -r requirements.txt.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install all the Python packages listed in the requirements file.
patterns.description_learner: pip install -r requirements.txt installs all packages from a requirements file. Run this after cloning a Python project.

patterns.description_noob: I'll open Python's interactive mode where you can type and test code line by line. Type exit() to quit.
patterns.description_learner: python3 with no arguments opens the REPL. Great for testing expressions. Ctrl+D or exit() to quit.

patterns.description_noob: I'll set up a new Node.js project in this folder — creates a package.json file.
patterns.description_learner: npm init -y creates package.json with defaults. -y skips the questions. Required for any npm project.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install a JavaScript library for your Node.js project.
patterns.description_learner: npm install adds a package to node_modules/ and records it in package.json dependencies.

patterns.description_noob: I'll start your Node.js project using the start script defined in package.json.
patterns.description_learner: npm start runs the 'start' script from package.json. npm run scriptname for other scripts.

patterns.description_noob: I'll count how many lines are in that file.
patterns.description_learner: wc -l counts newlines. wc -w counts words. wc -c counts bytes. wc alone gives all three.

patterns.description_noob: I'll sort all the lines in that file alphabetically.
patterns.description_learner: sort outputs lines in alphabetical order. -r reverse, -n numeric, -u unique (remove duplicates), -k field.

patterns.description_noob: I'll remove all duplicate lines from the file, keeping only unique ones.
patterns.description_learner: uniq removes consecutive duplicates, so sort first. uniq -c counts occurrences. uniq -d shows only duplicates.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show the first 20 lines of that file.
patterns.description_learner: head -n N shows first N lines. Default is 10. tail -n N shows last N lines. tail -f follows a growing file.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show the last 20 lines of that file — useful for reading log files.
patterns.description_learner: tail -n N shows last N lines. tail -f follows a growing file in real time (great for logs).

patterns.description_noob: I'll watch that log file and show new lines as they appear — live. Press Ctrl+C to stop.
patterns.description_learner: tail -f follows a file in real time. Great for watching server logs. Ctrl+C to stop.

patterns.description_noob: I'll create a file and write your text into it. Warning: this will overwrite if the file already exists.
patterns.description_learner: echo 'text' > file creates/overwrites. echo 'text' >> file appends. Use cat << EOF for multi-line.

patterns.description_noob: I'll add your text to the end of that file without overwriting what's already there.
patterns.description_learner: >> appends to file. > overwrites. echo 'text' >> file adds a line at the end.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show how large that file is.
patterns.description_learner: du -sh shows file/directory size. -s summary, -h human-readable. du -sh * shows all items in current dir.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show which ports on your device are currently listening for connections.
patterns.description_learner: ss -tlnp shows TCP listening ports. -t=TCP, -l=listening, -n=numeric, -p=process. Replaces netstat.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show all your network connections and their IP addresses.
patterns.description_learner: ip addr show lists all network interfaces. inet = IPv4, inet6 = IPv6. Look for wlan0 for WiFi.

patterns.description_noob: I'll make a request to that web address and show you what it returns.
patterns.description_learner: curl -s (silent) fetches a URL. Add | python3 -m json.tool to pretty-print JSON responses.

patterns.description_noob: I'll download that file and show you a progress bar.
patterns.description_learner: wget --show-progress displays a download bar. -c resumes interrupted downloads. -O renames the output file.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show all the environment settings that your programs can see.
patterns.description_learner: env shows all environment variables. echo $VARIABLE for a specific one. Common: $HOME, $PATH, $USER.
patterns.description_pro: env or printenv. echo $VAR for specific.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show which folders your device looks in when you type a command name.
patterns.description_learner: $PATH is a colon-separated list of directories searched for executables. Add to PATH in ~/.bashrc.

patterns.description_noob: I'll create a shortcut so you can type a short word instead of the full command.
patterns.description_learner: Adds alias to ~/.bashrc so it persists across sessions. source reloads bashrc immediately.

patterns.description_noob: I'll open your shell configuration file where you can add custom settings and shortcuts.
patterns.description_learner: ~/.bashrc runs on every new bash session. Add aliases, exports, PATH changes here. source ~/.bashrc to apply changes.

patterns.description_noob: I'll apply any changes you made to your shell settings without needing to restart Termux.
patterns.description_learner: source ~/.bashrc reloads your shell config. Equivalent to opening a new terminal session.

patterns.description_noob: I'll tell Android to keep Termux running even when your screen turns off — important for long tasks.
patterns.description_learner: termux-wake-lock acquires an Android wake lock. Prevents background kill. Release with termux-wake-unlock.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show your battery level and charging status.
patterns.description_learner: termux-battery-status returns JSON with percentage, status, plugged. Needs termux-api package.

patterns.description_noob: I'll send a notification to your phone from the terminal.
patterns.description_learner: termux-notification creates an Android notification. Needs termux-api. --ongoing keeps it persistent.

patterns.description_noob: I'll open the Android share menu so you can send that file to WhatsApp, email, Drive, etc.
patterns.description_learner: termux-share opens Android intent chooser for a file. Needs termux-api package.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install the Termux API package. You also need to install the 'Termux:API' companion app from F-Droid on your phone.
patterns.description_learner: termux-api package provides Android hardware access (battery, notifications, camera, etc). Requires Termux:API app from F-Droid too.

patterns.description_noob: I'll download that video. Make sure yt-dlp is installed first (pkg install yt-dlp).
patterns.description_learner: yt-dlp downloads from YouTube and 1000+ other sites. -x --audio-format mp3 for audio only. -f best for best quality.

patterns.description_noob: I'll download just the audio from that video as an MP3 file.
patterns.description_learner: yt-dlp -x extracts audio. --audio-format mp3 converts to MP3. Needs ffmpeg installed too.

patterns.description_noob: I'll convert that video file to a different format.
patterns.description_learner: ffmpeg -i input output. Format determined by output extension. Add -crf 28 to compress. -vn for audio only.

patterns.description_noob: I'll open a SQLite database (or create one if it doesn't exist yet).
patterns.description_learner: sqlite3 opens an interactive SQLite shell. .tables shows tables. .quit exits. .schema shows structure.

patterns.description_noob: I'll count how many words are in that file.
patterns.description_learner: wc -w counts words. wc -l lines. wc -c bytes. wc -m characters.

patterns.description_noob: I'll find every occurrence of that word in the file and replace it with the new one.
patterns.description_learner: sed -i in-place edit. s/old/new/g = substitute all occurrences. Without -i, prints to stdout instead.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show overall storage and how much your Termux home folder is using.
patterns.description_learner: df -h shows filesystem usage. du -sh ~ shows your home dir size. Combine for a full picture.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show you basic information about your device and system.
patterns.description_learner: uname -a = kernel info, free -h = RAM, df -h ~ = storage. Useful quick system overview.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install the tools needed to compile C and C++ code.
patterns.description_learner: clang is Termux's C/C++ compiler (gcc isn't available). make and cmake are build systems.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install proot-distro which lets you run a full Linux system (Ubuntu, Debian, etc.) inside Termux.
patterns.description_learner: proot-distro provides Linux distros in Termux via proot. Run: proot-distro install ubuntu, then proot-distro login ubuntu.

patterns.description_noob: I'll check if that tool is installed and show you where it's located.
patterns.description_learner: which shows the path of an executable. Exit code 0 = found, 1 = not found. command -v is more portable.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show the last 20 commands you typed.
patterns.description_learner: history shows all past commands. Pipe to tail to limit. Ctrl+R for interactive reverse search.

patterns.description_noob: I'll try to give you root access. Note: this requires your device to be rooted.
patterns.description_learner: tsu is Termux's sudo equivalent. Requires a rooted device. pkg install tsu first.

patterns.description_noob: I'll refresh the list of available packages. Run this if package installs are failing.
patterns.description_learner: pkg update refreshes the package index from Termux repos. Run before installing if you get 'not found' errors.

patterns.description_noob: I'll clean up old downloaded package files to free up space.
patterns.description_learner: pkg autoclean removes cached .deb files. pkg clean removes all cached files. Frees storage without removing packages.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show all the tools and packages currently installed in Termux.
patterns.description_learner: pkg list-installed shows all installed packages. Pipe to grep to filter: pkg list-installed | grep python.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show information about that package — what it does, its size, and what it needs.
patterns.description_learner: pkg show displays package metadata: version, size, dependencies, description.

patterns.description_noob: I'll take you back to your home folder.
patterns.description_learner: cd ~ navigates to $HOME (/data/data/com.termux/files/home in Termux).

patterns.description_noob: I'll move you up one folder level.
patterns.description_learner: cd .. navigates to the parent directory. cd ../.. goes up two levels.

patterns.description_noob: I'll open a text editor so you can write a new shell script.
patterns.description_learner: Creates a .sh file in nano. Start with #!/bin/bash on the first line. Ctrl+O save, Ctrl+X exit. Then chmod +x to run.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show you exactly which folder you're currently in.
patterns.description_learner: pwd = print working directory. Always run this if you're unsure where you are.

patterns.description_noob: I'll open that file in the Nano text editor. Ctrl+O to save, Ctrl+X to exit.
patterns.description_learner: nano filename opens for editing. Ctrl+W search, Ctrl+G help, Ctrl+K cut line, Ctrl+U paste.

patterns.description_noob: I'll extract the zip file into the current folder.
patterns.description_learner: unzip -d . extracts to current directory. Use -d foldername to extract into a specific folder.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show what files are inside the zip without extracting them.
patterns.description_learner: unzip -l lists archive contents without extracting. Useful for checking before extract.

patterns.description_noob: I'll create a zip file that requires a password to open.
patterns.description_learner: zip -e encrypts the archive with a password. You'll be prompted to set it.

patterns.description_noob: I'll check if a specific port is open and reachable on that address.
patterns.description_learner: nc -zv = netcat zero-I/O verbose. Tests TCP connectivity to host:port. Exit 0 = open.

patterns.description_noob: I'll copy a file to another computer over SSH.
patterns.description_learner: scp = secure copy over SSH. Same syntax as cp but with user@host:path for remote side.

patterns.description_noob: I'll send data to a web API using an HTTP POST request.
patterns.description_learner: curl -X POST sends POST. -H sets headers. -d is the data body. Use -s for silent mode.

patterns.description_noob: I'll run that command in the background so you can keep using the terminal.
patterns.description_learner: nohup prevents HUP signal from killing the process. & runs it in background. Output goes to nohup.out.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show any commands currently running in the background.
patterns.description_learner: jobs shows background processes. fg brings one to foreground. bg resumes stopped job. kill %N kills job N.

patterns.description_noob: I'll bring the background command back to the front so you can see it.
patterns.description_learner: fg resumes the most recent background/stopped job. fg %2 for specific job number.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show which GitHub/remote repositories this project is connected to.
patterns.description_learner: git remote -v shows all remotes with their fetch/push URLs. origin is the default remote name.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show all the branches in this project, including ones on GitHub.
patterns.description_learner: git branch -a shows local and remote branches. -r shows only remote. Active branch marked with *.

patterns.description_noob: I'll combine another branch's changes into your current branch.
patterns.description_learner: git merge integrates changes from another branch. Creates a merge commit. Resolve conflicts if they appear.

patterns.description_noob: I'll download the latest information from GitHub without changing your files.
patterns.description_learner: git fetch downloads remote changes without merging. Safer than pull. Then git merge or git rebase manually.

patterns.description_noob: I'll mark this point in your project history with a version label.
patterns.description_learner: git tag creates a lightweight tag. git tag -a v1.0 -m 'message' for annotated tag. git push --tags to push.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show which version of Python is installed.
patterns.description_learner: python3 --version shows the version. python3 -c 'import sys; print(sys.version)' for more detail.

patterns.description_noob: I'll save a list of all your Python packages so others can install them.
patterns.description_learner: pip freeze outputs installed packages in requirements format. Redirect to requirements.txt to save.

patterns.description_noob: I'll start your Flask web server so you can access it from your phone's browser.
patterns.description_learner: flask run starts dev server. --host=0.0.0.0 makes it accessible on local network. Default port 5000.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show this month's calendar.
patterns.description_learner: cal shows current month. cal 2025 shows full year. cal 12 2025 shows specific month.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show how long your device has been running since the last restart.
patterns.description_learner: uptime shows time since boot, logged in users, and load averages (1, 5, 15 minute).

patterns.description_noob: I'll generate a random strong password for you.
patterns.description_learner: openssl rand -base64 N generates N random bytes encoded as base64. Good for passwords and tokens.

patterns.description_noob: I'll calculate a unique fingerprint for that file to verify it hasn't been corrupted.
patterns.description_learner: sha256sum generates a SHA-256 hash. Compare with published hash to verify download integrity. md5sum for MD5.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install and open a network monitor to show which apps are using your data.
patterns.description_learner: nethogs shows per-process network usage in real time. Needs installation. q to quit.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show which files are currently open by running programs.
patterns.description_learner: lsof lists open files. lsof -i shows network connections. lsof -p PID shows files for specific process.

patterns.command: git config --global user.name "{name}" && git config --global user.email "{email}" && git config --global init.defaultBranch main
patterns.description_noob: I'll configure Git with your name, email, and set the default branch to 'main'. Fill in your details.
patterns.description_learner: Sets user.name, user.email, and defaultBranch globally. Required before first commit. Use your GitHub email.
patterns.example: git config --global user.name 'DeVen' && git config --global user.email 'me@email.com'

patterns.description_noob: I'll install Ruby — a programming language often used for web development.
patterns.description_learner: Termux package is 'ruby'. Includes gem package manager. gem install bundler after.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install Go — a fast, modern programming language made by Google.
patterns.description_learner: Termux package is 'golang'. Sets up GOPATH automatically. go run, go build, go install.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install Rust — a powerful systems programming language. Warning: this is a large download (~500MB).
patterns.description_learner: pkg install rust gives rustc compiler and cargo package manager. Large install. Use on mid/high tier devices.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install Java — needed for Android development and many Java programs.
patterns.description_learner: Termux has OpenJDK 21. Includes javac, java, jar. Use openjdk-17 for smaller footprint.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install PHP — a popular language for building websites.
patterns.description_learner: PHP for Termux. Run scripts: php script.php. Built-in server: php -S 0.0.0.0:8080.

patterns.description_noob: I'll start PHP's built-in web server. Open your browser to http://localhost:8080.
patterns.description_learner: php -S starts the built-in dev server. 0.0.0.0 makes it accessible on local network. Ctrl+C to stop.

patterns.description_noob: I'll run your JavaScript file with Node.js.
patterns.description_learner: node executes a JS file. node with no args opens interactive REPL. --inspect for debugger.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install MariaDB — a database server (the Termux version of MySQL).
patterns.description_learner: Termux uses MariaDB instead of MySQL. After install: mysql_install_db, then mysqld_safe & to start.

patterns.description_noob: I'll install Redis — a fast in-memory data storage system.
patterns.description_learner: pkg install redis. Start: redis-server. Connect: redis-cli. Default port 6379.

patterns.description_noob: I'll create a complete backup of your Termux setup and save it to your Downloads folder.
patterns.description_learner: Archives the Termux home and usr directories. Date-stamped filename. Restore: tar -xzf backup.tar.gz -C /data/data/com.termux/files/

patterns.description_noob: I'll install figlet — it turns text into big ASCII art letters in the terminal.
patterns.description_learner: figlet renders text as ASCII art. figlet 'Hello' or pipe: echo 'Hello' | figlet. -f font to change style.

patterns.description_noob: I'll open a menu to pick a faster package download server near you.
patterns.description_learner: termux-change-repo lets you select a mirror. Pick one geographically close for faster pkg installs.

patterns.description_noob: I'll show all running processes in a tree format showing which started which.
patterns.description_learner: ps aux --forest shows process hierarchy. Parent-child relationships visible. pstree (pkg install pstree) is cleaner.

patterns.description_noob: I'll watch this folder and refresh every 2 seconds to show any new or changed files.
patterns.description_learner: watch -n N runs a command every N seconds. Ctrl+C to stop. Good for monitoring build output or log dirs.

patterns.description_noob: I'll encode that text to Base64 format.
patterns.description_learner: base64 encodes to Base64. base64 -d decodes. Pipe: echo 'text' | base64 or base64 file.

patterns.description_noob: I'll create a shortcut that points to another file or folder.
patterns.description_learner: ln -s creates a symbolic link. The link_name points to target. Useful for aliasing long paths.
